Symptoms: Olive-green to brown velvety or scab-like lesions on upper leaf surface | Dark, sooty, circular spots with feathery margins on leaves | Yellowing of leaf tissue surrounding lesions | Premature defoliation in severe infections | Raised, corky, dark scab lesions on fruit surface | Distorted or cracked fruit skin | Black, slightly raised spots on young shoots and twigs | Infected leaves may curl and drop early
Prevention: Plant scab-resistant apple varieties (Liberty, Freedom, Enterprise) | Rake and destroy fallen leaves before spring to eliminate overwintering spores | Prune trees to open canopy and improve air circulation | Avoid overhead irrigation — use drip irrigation to keep foliage dry | Remove mummified or infected fruits from tree and ground | Maintain orchard sanitation — clear debris after each season | Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilisation that encourages dense susceptible growth | Remove and destroy any visibly infected leaves or shoots at the very first sign
Treatment: Captan (50 WP) at 2.5 g/L — apply every 7–10 days during wet periods | Mancozeb (Dithane M-45 75 WP) at 2.5 g/L — broad contact fungicide | Myclobutanil (Rally 40 WP) at 0.4 g/L — systemic, curative up to 96 hrs post-infection | Difenoconazole (Score 250 EC) at 0.5 mL/L — highly systemic DMI fungicide | Trifloxystrobin + Tebuconazole (Nativo 75 WG) at 0.5 g/L — broad-spectrum systemic | Kresoxim-methyl (Stroby WG) at 0.15 g/L — strobilurin with curative activity | Lime-sulfur spray at dormant stage to kill overwintering spores on bark | Rotate fungicide classes each application to prevent resistance build-up

Symptoms: Circular, purple to brown leaf spots with lighter tan centers (frog-eye appearance) | Leaf spots may have a yellow halo around the lesion | Small, black pycnidia (fungal fruiting bodies) visible within leaf spots | Cankers on branches — orange-brown, sunken, elliptical with rough cracked bark | Fruit rot starting as small purple spots enlarging to firm, black, mummified fruit | Alternating black and brown concentric rings on infected fruit | Mummified black fruit may remain attached to tree | Twig dieback with reddish-brown discoloration under bark
Prevention: Remove and destroy mummified fruits from trees and orchard floor | Prune dead or diseased wood at least 15 cm below visible infection | Disinfect pruning tools with 70% alcohol between cuts to avoid spreading | Remove nearby wild or abandoned apple and crabapple trees | Ensure good air circulation through proper tree spacing | Remove and dispose of any infected leaves or fruit at first sign of disease | Avoid wounding trees during pruning — wounds are entry points for the fungus | Plant less-susceptible apple varieties in areas with high disease history
Treatment: Captan (50 WP) at 2.5 g/L — apply every 10–14 days from pink stage through harvest | Mancozeb (Dithane M-45) at 2.5 g/L — contact protectant, ensure full coverage | Thiophanate-methyl (Topsin-M 70 WP) at 1 g/L — systemic benzimidazole fungicide | Trifloxystrobin (Flint 50 WG) at 0.1 g/L — strobilurin effective on fruit and foliage | Copper oxychloride (50 WP) at 3 g/L — effective on canker lesions on branches | Bordeaux mixture (1%) — spray on cankers during early season | Ziram (76 DF) at 3 g/L — contact fungicide in rotation programs | Apply fertiliser to maintain tree vigour and support recovery

Symptoms: Bright yellow-orange circular spots on upper leaf surface in spring | Lesions enlarge; orange, tube-like aecia (spore horns) appear on lower leaf surface | Yellow halos surrounding rust lesions on upper surface | Distorted or puckered leaf tissue around infection sites | Pale yellow to orange spots on fruit surface, later developing tube-like structures | Premature defoliation in heavily infected trees | Twig and shoot infections causing swelling and dieback | On alternate host (cedar/juniper): orange gelatinous telial horns in spring
Prevention: Plant cedar-apple rust resistant apple varieties (Redfree, Pristine, Gold Rush) | Remove eastern red cedar and ornamental juniper trees near the orchard | Avoid planting apples near cedar or juniper windbreaks | Monitor nearby cedars in spring for orange gelatinous galls — treat immediately when seen | Prune out infected apple shoots and twigs showing rust symptoms early | Maintain good tree spacing to reduce humidity and leaf wetness | Remove infected leaves promptly at first appearance to slow spread
Treatment: Myclobutanil (Rally 40 WP) at 0.4 g/L — most effective DMI for rust; apply at pink bud | Propiconazole (Tilt 250 EC) at 1 mL/L — systemic triazole, apply at early infection | Triadimefon (Bayleton 25 WP) at 1 g/L — triazole fungicide, good rust control | Fenarimol (Rubigan 12 EC) at 0.5 mL/L — highly effective against rust diseases | Trifloxystrobin + Tebuconazole (Nativo) at 0.5 g/L — combined systemic control | Wettable sulfur at 3 g/L — cost-effective option when temperature is below 32°C | Mancozeb (75 WP) at 2.5 g/L — contact protectant applied every 7–10 days | Foliar micronutrient spray (potassium, zinc) to restore leaf health after infection

Symptoms: White to gray powdery fungal growth on upper and lower leaf surfaces | Affected leaves curl, pucker, and distort inward | Young leaves and shoots most severely affected; stunted growth | Reddish or purplish discoloration on infected young tissue | Premature leaf drop in severe infections | White powdery coating on young shoots and petioles | Infected fruit may show russeting or surface distortion | Black cleistothecia (fungal overwintering bodies) visible in late season
Prevention: Plant powdery mildew-resistant cherry varieties | Prune infected shoots in late winter before bud break to remove overwintering mycelium | Improve air circulation through thinning and open-centre pruning | Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilisation — lush growth is highly susceptible | Remove and destroy infected prunings — do not compost them | Remove visibly infected leaves and shoots at first sign to prevent spread | Avoid shaded, poorly ventilated planting sites | Water at soil level — avoid wetting foliage
Treatment: Wettable sulfur (80 WP) at 3 g/L — effective and affordable; do not use above 32°C | Myclobutanil (Rally 40 WP) at 0.4 g/L — systemic DMI with curative action | Tebuconazole (Folicur 250 EW) at 1 mL/L — systemic triazole, strong curative activity | Trifloxystrobin (Flint 50 WG) at 0.1 g/L — strobilurin with excellent PM efficacy | Potassium bicarbonate (Kaligreen) at 5 g/L — organic, disrupts fungal cell membrane | Neem oil (0.03 EC) at 5 mL/L — organic spray for moderate infections | Quinoxyfen (Arius 250 SC) at 0.5 mL/L — specific powdery mildew fungicide | Apply every 7–14 days; rotate fungicide groups to prevent resistance

Symptoms: Small, rectangular to elongated tan to gray lesions on leaves, running parallel to leaf veins | Lesions bounded by leaf veins giving a rectangular blocky appearance | Lesion centers turn gray to tan with darker brown borders as they age | Multiple lesions coalesce to form large necrotic areas on leaf blade | Severely infected leaves turn completely brown and die prematurely | Gray sporulation (conidia) visible on lesion surfaces under humid conditions | Lesions appear first on lower leaves and progress upward | Affected plants show early senescence and reduced photosynthesis
Prevention: Plant GLS-resistant or tolerant maize hybrid varieties | Rotate crops — avoid growing maize continuously on the same field | Deep plough or disc crop residues after harvest to reduce inoculum on soil surface | Maintain proper plant spacing to reduce canopy humidity | Reduce irrigation frequency to avoid prolonged leaf wetness | Avoid planting susceptible varieties in fields with a history of severe GLS | Remove and destroy lower infected leaves at first sign of rectangular grey lesions | Early planting helps canopy develop before peak disease pressure period
Treatment: Azoxystrobin (Amistar 25 SC) at 1 mL/L — apply at VT to R1 growth stage | Propiconazole (Tilt 250 EC) at 1 mL/L — effective triazole for GLS control | Pyraclostrobin (Headline EC) at 1.5 mL/L — strobilurin with plant health benefits | Trifloxystrobin + Propiconazole (Stratego 250 EC) at 1 mL/L — combination product | Tebuconazole (Folicur 250 EW) at 1 mL/L — systemic triazole | Mancozeb (Dithane M-45 75 WP) at 2.5 g/L — contact protectant every 10 days | Foliar fertiliser (NPK + micronutrients) to support plant recovery | Second application 14 days after first if disease pressure remains high

Symptoms: Small, circular to elongated powdery pustules (uredia) on both leaf surfaces | Pustules are cinnamon-brown to brick-red, scattered across entire leaf blade | Pustules rupture through epidermis releasing powdery reddish-brown urediniospores | Later in season pustules turn dark brown-black as teliospores form | Heavy infection causes leaves to turn yellow and die prematurely | Pustules may also appear on husks, leaf sheaths, and stalks | Entire field may appear rust-colored in severe outbreaks | Infected leaves feel rough or powdery when rubbed
Prevention: Plant rust-resistant maize hybrid varieties | Early planting to avoid peak rust spore dispersal periods | Destroy volunteer maize plants that harbour rust spores between seasons | Rotate crops to reduce local inoculum build-up | Maintain adequate potassium nutrition — deficiency increases susceptibility | Scout fields regularly from V6 growth stage for early pustule detection | Remove and destroy infected lower leaves as soon as first pustules appear | Avoid planting susceptible open-pollinated varieties in rust-prone regions
Treatment: Propiconazole (Tilt 250 EC) at 1 mL/L — apply at first pustule appearance | Azoxystrobin (Amistar 25 SC) at 1 mL/L — strobilurin, apply before heavy infection | Tebuconazole (Folicur 250 EW) at 1 mL/L — systemic triazole with curative activity | Pyraclostrobin + Metconazole (Twinline) at 1 mL/L — mixed mode of action | Trifloxystrobin (Flint 50 WG) at 0.1 g/L — strobilurin for early-stage rust | Mancozeb (75 WP) at 2.5 g/L — contact protectant, apply every 7–10 days | Spray at VT–R1 stage for maximum protection during grain fill | Repeat application after 14 days if disease pressure remains high

Symptoms: Large, elliptical to cigar-shaped gray-green to tan lesions 2.5–15 cm long on leaves | Lesions have wavy, irregular, pale tan to gray centers with darker borders | Dark olive-gray sporulation visible in center of lesions under humid conditions | Lesions run parallel to leaf veins and may coalesce in heavy infections | Lower leaves affected first; disease progresses upward | Severely blighted plants show premature death of entire leaf blade | Stalk rot may follow severe foliar infection due to reduced photosynthate | Husks may show lesions in heavily infected fields

Symptoms: Small, circular, tan to brown leaf spots with dark brown borders | Black pycnidia (tiny black dots) arranged in concentric rings within leaf lesions | Infected berries turn brown then rapidly shrivel into hard black mummies | Salmon or cream-colored spore masses on berry surface early in infection | Infected berries have a wrinkled, raisin-like appearance before turning jet black | Lesions on shoot tips and tendrils — dark brown, elongated, sunken cankers | Petiole and rachis lesions that can cause cluster death | Mummified berries remain attached to cluster and serve as primary inoculum source
Prevention: Remove and destroy all mummified berries, infected canes, and fallen leaves | Prune vines to open canopy and improve air circulation | Position shoots upright for rapid leaf drying after rain | Remove wild grapes near vineyard that serve as inoculum reservoirs | Cultivate soil to bury infected debris and reduce spore release | Immediately remove and dispose of any infected leaves or berry clusters at first sign | Avoid overhead irrigation — use drip systems | Scout weekly from budbreak through veraison

Treatment: Mancozeb (Dithane M-45 75 WP) at 2.5 g/L — apply every 7–10 days | Myclobutanil (Rally 40 WP) at 0.4 g/L — systemic DMI, active up to 96 hrs post-infection | Trifloxystrobin (Flint 50 WG) at 0.1 g/L — excellent curative and residual activity | Tebuconazole (Folicur 250 EW) at 1 mL/L — effective post-infection control | Azoxystrobin (Quadris 25 SC) at 1 mL/L — strobilurin protectant | Captan (50 WP) at 2.5 g/L — broad-spectrum contact fungicide | Ziram (76 DF) at 3.5 g/L — contact protectant in rotation | Critical spray timings: pre-bloom, full bloom, and 4–5 weeks post-bloom

Symptoms: Interveinal chlorosis (yellowing between veins) with tiger-stripe pattern on leaves | Leaf margins and interveinal tissue turn red-purple in red varieties, yellow in white varieties | Necrotic leaf margins and eventual complete leaf browning and drop | Apoplexy — sudden complete wilting and death of entire vine in summer heat | Irregular dark brown to black spots or streaks on berry surface (measles symptoms) | Internal wood discoloration — brown to black streaking in cross-section of cordons | White rot (white spongy wood decay) in trunk and cordons | Stunted shoot growth; shortened internodes on affected canes
Prevention: Prune during dry weather to minimise infection risk | Apply wound sealants (Topsin paste or Bordeaux paste) immediately after every pruning cut | Use double-pruning technique — leave a spur stub first, remove in spring | Disinfect pruning tools with 10% sodium hypochlorite or 70% alcohol between vines | Plant certified disease-free nursery stock only | Remove and destroy severely infected vines including stumps and roots | Maintain adequate irrigation — water stress worsens apoplexy (sudden vine collapse) | Perform trunk renewal surgery to replace infected cordons with healthy new arms
Treatment: Trichoderma-based biocontrol (T. atroviride, T. asperellum) applied to pruning wounds | Flusilazole (Punch 40 EC) at 0.5 mL/L — may slow wood pathogen progression | Wound protectants: Vitiseal or LalSeal applied within 30 minutes of pruning | Bacillus subtilis (Serenade) as biological wound protectant spray | Balanced fertiliser programme (NPK + micronutrients) to maintain vine vigour | Foliar calcium spray to strengthen cell walls and slow symptom progression | Regular irrigation management to reduce heat stress that triggers apoplexy | Note: No fully curative chemical exists — management aims to slow progression

Symptoms: Irregular, angular brown to reddish-brown spots on upper leaf surface | Lesions bounded by larger veins giving angular appearance | Dark brown concentric rings or zones within lesion | Grayish-white sporulation (conidia) visible on lower leaf surface under humid conditions | Lesions may coalesce causing large necrotic patches and early defoliation | Infected berries develop brown lesions and may crack or rot | Lesions on rachis and petioles causing cluster or leaf drop | Symptoms more severe on lower, older leaves first
Prevention: Remove and destroy infected fallen leaves to reduce overwintering inoculum | Prune to improve air circulation within the vine canopy | Train vines to vertical shoot positioning for better canopy airflow | Avoid wetting foliage with overhead irrigation | Remove and dispose of infected leaves and rachis at first sign of angular brown spots | Balanced fertilisation — avoid excess nitrogen which causes dense susceptible growth | Scout vineyards after rainy periods for early detection
Treatment: Copper oxychloride (50 WP) at 3 g/L — effective contact fungicide, apply after rain | Mancozeb (Dithane M-45 75 WP) at 2.5 g/L — broad protectant every 10 days | Carbendazim (50 WP) at 1 g/L — systemic benzimidazole fungicide | Tebuconazole (Folicur 250 EW) at 1 mL/L — systemic triazole control | Azoxystrobin (Amistar 25 SC) at 1 mL/L — strobilurin with translaminar movement | Chlorothalonil (Bravo 720) at 2 mL/L — contact fungicide, good residual activity | Bordeaux mixture (1%) — traditional copper spray, organic-programme approved | Repeat every 10–14 days during wet conditions until disease is controlled

Symptoms: Asymmetric, blotchy mottling of leaves — yellow and green patches on same leaf (huanglongbing signature sign) | Yellowing of one or few shoots while rest of tree remains green (shoot dieback) | Lopsided, small, misshapen fruit with green color retained at stylar end | Bitter, salty taste of infected fruit; internal segments abort | Inverted color — fruit remains green at bottom (stylar end) instead of top | Abscission of fruit before maturity — heavy fruit drop | Corky veins on affected leaves — veins appear thickened and yellow | Zinc and iron deficiency-like symptoms (due to phloem blockage, not actual deficiency) | Dieback of branches; overall tree decline and death within years if untreated
Prevention: Use only certified disease-free budwood and rootstocks from licensed nurseries | Establish nurseries under insect-proof screen houses (50-mesh) | Control Asian citrus psyllid (Diaphorina citri) — the insect vector — from the nursery stage | Remove and destroy any tree showing blotchy mottled leaves or lopsided fruit immediately | Create psyllid-free buffer zones around nurseries | Use reflective silver mulch around young trees to deter psyllid landing | Avoid planting near abandoned or neglected citrus orchards | Inspect new plantings every 2 weeks for psyllid colonies and early HLB symptoms
Treatment: Imidacloprid (Confidor 200 SL) at 0.5 mL/L — systemic insecticide to control psyllid vector | Thiamethoxam (Actara 25 WG) at 0.3 g/L — systemic neonicotinoid for psyllid suppression | Spirotetramat (Movento 150 OD) at 1.5 mL/L — phloem-mobile, controls psyllid nymphs | Soil drench with Imidacloprid for long-term systemic psyllid control | Oxytetracycline trunk injection (experimental) — suppresses symptoms and extends tree life | Micronutrient foliar sprays (zinc, manganese, boron) to manage deficiency symptoms | Intensive fertilisation (NPK + chelated micronutrients) to prolong productive tree life | Note: No cure exists — treatment focuses on psyllid control and tree nutrition management

Symptoms: Small, water-soaked spots on leaves that turn purple to dark brown | Angular leaf lesions with yellow halos that later fall out leaving shot-holes | Defoliation from severe infections — leaves drop prematurely | Sunken, water-soaked lesions on fruit surface that turn brown and crack | Fruit lesions exude bacterial ooze; become corky, rough, and scabby | Cracking and pitting of fruit surface reducing marketability | Dark, water-soaked cankers on twigs and young shoots | Spring cankers on previous year's wood oozing bacterial exudate
Prevention: Plant bacterial spot-resistant peach varieties (Contender, Redhaven, Reliance) | Avoid sites with heavy prevailing winds that cause leaf wounds | Establish windbreaks on the windward side of the orchard | Prune to open canopy and improve air movement through trees | Avoid overhead irrigation — use drip systems | Remove and destroy infected twigs, leaves, and cankers at first sign | Avoid excessive nitrogen which promotes dense, succulent, susceptible growth | Apply dormant copper sprays to reduce overwintering bacterial inoculum on bark
Treatment: Copper hydroxide (Kocide 3000 WG) at 2 g/L — apply at shuck split and petal fall | Copper oxychloride (50 WP) at 3 g/L — protectant, repeat throughout the season | Oxytetracycline (Mycoshield) at 0.5 g/L — antibiotic bactericide during bloom | Kasugamycin (Kasumin 2L) at 1 mL/L — antibiotic effective against Xanthomonas | Zinc sulfate (0.5%) + Copper sulfate (0.5%) — combination bactericide spray | Bordeaux mixture (1%) — traditional copper bactericide | Balanced NPK fertiliser to improve tree vigour and wound recovery | Limit copper applications per season to avoid phytotoxicity

Symptoms: Small, water-soaked, greasy-looking circular spots on leaves | Lesions turn brown to black with yellow halos around them | Center of lesions may dry out and fall away leaving ragged shot-holes | Infected leaves turn yellow and drop — severe defoliation in wet weather | Raised, scabby, brown to black lesions on fruit surface | Fruit lesions may crack and allow secondary rot pathogens to enter | Stem lesions — dark brown cankers on young petioles and stems | Heavily infected seedlings show wilting and damping-off symptoms
Prevention: Use only certified disease-free, treated seed | Treat seeds with hot water at 50°C for 25 minutes before sowing | Plant resistant bell pepper varieties (Aristotle, Revolution, Tidal Wave) | Practice 2–3 year crop rotation with non-solanaceous crops | Avoid overhead irrigation — use drip systems to keep foliage dry | Do not work in fields when foliage is wet — reduces mechanical spread | Remove and destroy infected leaves and stems at first sign of water-soaked spots | Sanitise transplant trays and all equipment with 1% bleach solution
Treatment: Copper hydroxide (Kocide 3000) at 2 g/L — apply every 5–7 days during wet weather | Copper oxychloride (50 WP) at 3 g/L — broad-spectrum bactericide | Copper + Mancozeb combination (Curzate CM) — better efficacy than copper alone | Kasugamycin (Kasumin 2L) at 1 mL/L — antibiotic against Xanthomonas strains | Streptomycin sulfate (Agri-Mycin 17) at 0.5 g/L — antibiotic spray | Acibenzolar-S-methyl (Actigard 50 WG) at 0.05 g/L — activates plant systemic resistance | Bordeaux mixture (1%) — organic-approved copper spray | Balanced fertiliser (NPK) to maintain plant vigour during recovery

Symptoms: Circular to angular dark brown to black lesions with concentric rings (target-board appearance) | Lesions surrounded by yellow chlorotic halo on leaf | Lesions first appear on older, lower leaves and progress upward | Lesion centers may dry and fall out in papery texture | Dark brown, sunken, leathery lesions on tuber surface with dry rot underneath | Stem lesions — dark brown, elongated, slightly sunken cankers near soil line | Premature defoliation reducing tuber yield significantly | Black, powdery sporulation visible in concentric rings within lesions under humid conditions
Prevention: Plant certified disease-free seed tubers | Use early blight-tolerant potato varieties | Practice 3-year crop rotation with non-solanaceous crops | Maintain balanced NPK soil fertility — well-nourished plants resist infection better | Avoid drought stress — water-stressed plants are far more susceptible | Hill up soil around plants to protect tubers and lower stems | Remove and destroy lower infected leaves immediately at first sign of target-board lesions | Avoid late-afternoon irrigation — water in the morning so foliage dries quickly
Treatment: Mancozeb (Dithane M-45 75 WP) at 2.5 g/L — apply every 7–10 days from first symptoms | Chlorothalonil (Bravo 720 SC) at 2 mL/L — contact fungicide, excellent early blight control | Azoxystrobin (Amistar 25 SC) at 1 mL/L — systemic strobilurin | Difenoconazole (Score 250 EC) at 0.5 mL/L — highly systemic DMI fungicide | Pyraclostrobin (Cabrio EG) at 1.5 g/L — strobilurin with plant health benefits | Iprodione (Rovral 50 WP) at 1.5 g/L — dicarboximide fungicide | Copper oxychloride (50 WP) at 3 g/L — contact option for organic programmes | Foliar NPK + micronutrient spray to support recovery and maintain tuber yield

Symptoms: Pale green to dark brown water-soaked lesions appearing rapidly on leaf margins and tips | Lesions enlarge quickly; white fluffy sporulation on underside of leaves in humid conditions | Dark brown to black oily-looking patches that spread rapidly across entire leaf | Infected stems show dark brown to black lesions causing stem collapse | Entire field can appear blighted within days under favorable conditions | Infected tubers show brown to reddish-brown granular rot extending into flesh | Foul smell from decaying infected tissue in field and storage | Young plants may show complete collapse (damping-off) in severe early infections
Prevention: Plant certified disease-free seed potatoes | Use late blight-resistant potato varieties (Sarpo Mira, Defender, Jacqueline Lee) | Avoid planting near cull piles or volunteer potato and tomato plants | Hill potatoes adequately to protect tubers from sporangia washing into soil | Use drip irrigation — avoid wetting foliage at any time | Destroy haulm (vine) 2–3 weeks before harvest to prevent tuber infection | Remove any plant showing first water-soaked lesions immediately before the disease spreads | Ensure cool, dry, ventilated storage conditions to prevent post-harvest rot
Treatment: Metalaxyl + Mancozeb (Ridomil Gold MZ 68 WG) at 2.5 g/L — systemic + contact, gold standard | Cymoxanil + Mancozeb (Curzate M8 72 WP) at 2.5 g/L — curative within 2–3 days post-infection | Dimethomorph + Mancozeb (Acrobat MZ WP) at 2 g/L — phenylamide combination | Mandipropamid (Revus 250 SC) at 0.6 mL/L — highly systemic | Fluopicolide + Propamocarb (Infinito SC) at 1.6 mL/L — dual systemic action | Chlorothalonil (Bravo 720) at 2 mL/L — contact protectant in rotation | Copper hydroxide (Kocide 3000) at 2 g/L — organic-approved option | Apply every 5–7 days in wet conditions; strictly rotate modes of action each spray

Prevention: Plant certified disease-free seed potatoes | Use late blight-resistant potato varieties (Sarpo Mira, Defender, Jacqueline Lee) | Avoid planting near cull piles or volunteer potato and tomato plants | Hill potatoes adequately to protect tubers from sporangia washing into soil | Use drip irrigation — avoid wetting foliage at any time | Destroy haulm (vine) 2–3 weeks before harvest to prevent tuber infection | Remove any plant showing first water-soaked lesions immediately before the disease spreads | Ensure cool, dry, ventilated storage conditions to prevent post-harvest rot

Symptoms: White to gray powdery fungal colonies on upper and lower leaf surfaces | Circular powdery spots that enlarge and merge to cover entire leaf surface | Affected leaves turn yellow, then brown and necrotic | Powdery coating on petioles, stems, and occasionally fruit surface | Young leaves most susceptible — severely infected leaves curl and distort | Premature leaf senescence and defoliation exposing fruit to sunscald | Reduced fruit set and undersized fruit due to loss of photosynthetic leaf area | Black cleistothecia (fruiting bodies) visible on white mycelium in late season
Prevention: Plant powdery mildew-resistant squash varieties | Maintain adequate plant spacing for good airflow — avoid dense planting | Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilisation which produces lush, susceptible growth | Water at soil level in the morning — never wet the foliage | Orient rows parallel to prevailing winds to improve air movement | Use reflective mulches to reduce disease-favouring microclimate | Remove and dispose of first infected leaves immediately to prevent spore spread | Remove and destroy all infected plant debris at the end of each season
Treatment: Wettable sulfur (80 WP) at 3 g/L — most cost-effective option; do not apply above 32°C | Myclobutanil (Rally 40 WP) at 0.4 g/L — systemic DMI, curative and preventive | Azoxystrobin (Amistar 25 SC) at 1 mL/L — strobilurin, excellent preventive activity | Trifloxystrobin (Flint 50 WG) at 0.1 g/L — highly effective against cucurbit powdery mildew | Potassium bicarbonate (Kaligreen 85 WP) at 5 g/L — organic, kills spores on contact | Neem oil (1–2%) — organic spray that suffocates fungal mycelium | Bupirimate (Nimrod 250 EC) at 0.5 mL/L — specific powdery mildew fungicide | Apply every 7–10 days from first sign; rotate fungicide groups to prevent resistance

Symptoms: Irregular, purple to dark brown blotches on upper leaf surface without distinct margins | Lesion centers turn gray to light brown while borders remain dark purple | Entire leaf may turn reddish-brown giving scorched appearance | Lesions coalesce to cover large portions of leaf blade | Infected leaves wither, dry out, and persist on plant giving burned appearance | Small, circular black pycnidia visible in lesion centers | Purplish-brown lesions on petioles, stolons, and calyx of fruit | Severely infected plants show stunting and reduced runner production
Prevention: Plant only certified disease-free runners from reputable nurseries | Use resistant strawberry varieties where available | Maintain proper plant spacing to promote airflow between plants | Avoid overhead irrigation — use drip irrigation | Remove and destroy infected leaves and debris during the growing season at first sign | Renovate strawberry beds after fruiting — mow foliage and remove all debris | Practice 2–3 year rotation before replanting strawberries in the same bed | Avoid low-lying, poorly drained areas where moisture and humidity accumulate

Symptoms: Small, circular to angular water-soaked spots on leaves turning brown to black | Lesions with yellow halos on leaves; centers may dry out and fall away (shot-hole) | Greasy, dark brown to black spots on fruit surface — raised and scabby texture | Fruit lesions crack and allow secondary pathogens entry | Defoliation — infected leaves yellow and drop, exposing fruit to sunscald | Dark, water-soaked lesions on stems and petioles | Bacterial ooze from stem lesions in humid conditions | Seedling damping-off and wilting in severe nursery infections
Prevention: Use only certified disease-free seed and seedlings | Treat seeds with hot water at 50°C for 25 minutes before sowing | Plant resistant tomato varieties (Juliet, Mountain Magic, Plum Crimson) | Practice 2–3 year crop rotation with non-solanaceous crops | Avoid overhead irrigation — use drip systems | Do not handle plants when foliage is wet to avoid mechanical spread | Remove and destroy infected leaves, stems, and fruit at first sign of water-soaked spots | Remove and destroy all crop residues immediately after harvest

Treatment: Chlorothalonil (Bravo 720 SC) at 2 mL/L — contact protectant, apply every 7 days | Mancozeb (Dithane M-45 75 WP) at 2.5 g/L — broad-spectrum contact fungicide | Azoxystrobin (Amistar 25 SC) at 1 mL/L — systemic strobilurin | Difenoconazole (Score 250 EC) at 0.5 mL/L — highly systemic DMI fungicide | Pyraclostrobin + Boscalid (Pristine WG) at 0.7 g/L — dual-mode systemic | Trifloxystrobin + Tebuconazole (Nativo 75 WG) at 0.5 g/L — broad-spectrum | Copper oxychloride (50 WP) at 3 g/L — organic-programme copper option | Foliar potassium + calcium spray to strengthen leaf tissue during recovery

Prevention: Plant certified disease-free transplants | Use early blight-tolerant or resistant tomato varieties | Rotate crops — avoid tomato and potato family in same bed for 2–3 years | Use mulch to prevent soil splash from reaching lower leaves | Stake or cage plants to keep foliage off the ground | Water at base of plant — never wet the foliage | Remove lower infected leaves immediately at first sign of target-board lesions | Maintain adequate potassium and phosphorus nutrition to boost plant resistance

Symptoms: Pale green to dark brown, water-soaked, oily lesions rapidly appearing on leaf margins and tips | White cotton-like sporulation on undersides of leaves in humid/cool conditions | Entire leaf blade turns brown-black and shrivels rapidly | Brown to black stem lesions causing stem collapse and plant death | Green, oily-looking fruit lesions turning brown and firm, spreading across fruit surface | Infected fruit remains firm unlike bacterial rots — surface becomes brown and leathery | Distinctive unpleasant musty odor from infected fields | Entire plants collapse and fields appear as if frosted overnight in severe epidemics
Prevention: Plant late blight-resistant tomato varieties (Defiant PhR, Iron Lady, Mountain Merit) | Use certified disease-free transplants from screened nurseries | Avoid planting near potato fields, cull piles, or volunteer solanaceous plants | Use drip irrigation — never wet the foliage | Improve air circulation through pruning lower leaves and staking plants | Remove any plant showing first oily dark lesions immediately before spread to others | Harvest fruit before heavy rain events to reduce post-harvest loss | Destroy all haulm 2–3 weeks before harvest to prevent tuber/fruit infection
Treatment: Metalaxyl + Mancozeb (Ridomil Gold MZ 68 WG) at 2.5 g/L — systemic + contact, gold standard | Cymoxanil + Mancozeb (Curzate M8 72 WP) at 2.5 g/L — curative 2–3 days post-infection | Mandipropamid (Revus 250 SC) at 0.6 mL/L — highly systemic, excellent control | Dimethomorph (Acrobat 50 WP) at 1 g/L — phenylamide, mobile in leaf tissue | Fluopicolide + Propamocarb (Infinito SC) at 1.6 mL/L — dual systemic action | Chlorothalonil (Bravo 720 SC) at 2 mL/L — contact protectant in rotation | Copper hydroxide (Kocide 3000) at 2 g/L — organic-approved option | Apply every 5–7 days in cool wet weather; rotate modes of action every spray

Symptoms: Pale greenish-yellow spots on upper leaf surface, diffuse and irregular | Olive-green to gray-purple velvety mold on lower leaf surface corresponding to upper spots | Spots coalesce, leaves turn yellow then brown and wither | Older leaves affected first; disease progresses upward | Severely infected leaves curl upward, dry out, and drop | In rare cases, velvety mold may appear on stems, blossoms, and fruit surface | Fruit infection appears as irregular, dark green to gray patches that turn brown and leathery | Disease spreads rapidly in high-humidity environments like greenhouses
Prevention: Plant leaf mold-resistant tomato varieties (most modern hybrids carry Cf resistance genes) | Maintain greenhouse or field humidity below 85% through ventilation | Increase plant spacing to improve airflow within the canopy | Avoid overhead watering — irrigate in the morning at soil level only | Reduce night temperature fluctuations to prevent condensation on leaf surfaces | Remove and destroy infected leaves immediately at first sign of yellow spots | Disinfect greenhouse structures between crop cycles with bleach or copper sulfate | Monitor daily in humid conditions — disease spreads extremely rapidly
Treatment: Chlorothalonil (Bravo 720 SC) at 2 mL/L — contact fungicide, apply every 7 days | Mancozeb (Dithane M-45 75 WP) at 2.5 g/L — broad-spectrum protectant spray | Azoxystrobin (Amistar 25 SC) at 1 mL/L — systemic strobilurin with curative activity | Difenoconazole (Score 250 EC) at 0.5 mL/L — systemic DMI fungicide | Myclobutanil (Rally 40 WP) at 0.4 g/L — triazole with curative action | Trifloxystrobin + Tebuconazole (Nativo 75 WG) at 0.5 g/L — broad-spectrum systemic | Tebuconazole (Folicur 250 EW) at 1 mL/L — effective triazole | Apply every 7 days strictly in humid conditions; rotate fungicide groups each spray

Symptoms: Numerous small (2–4 mm) circular spots with dark brown borders and gray-white centers | Tiny black pycnidia (spore-producing structures) visible in lesion centers | Lesions surrounded by yellow halo | Lower, older leaves affected first — disease progresses upward rapidly | Heavy infection causes complete yellowing and premature defoliation | Defoliation exposes fruit to sunscald | Lesions may appear on stems and petioles — dark brown, elongated | Unlike early blight, spots are much smaller and lack concentric ring pattern
Prevention: Use certified disease-free seed and transplants | Rotate crops — minimum 2 years out of tomatoes or potato family | Apply mulch at soil surface to prevent spore splash from soil to lower leaves | Stake or cage plants to keep foliage off the ground | Avoid overhead irrigation — use drip or furrow irrigation | Remove lower infected leaves immediately as soon as white-centred spots appear | Do not work among wet plants — spores spread easily through water and contact | Remove and destroy all crop debris after harvest
Treatment: Chlorothalonil (Bravo 720 SC) at 2 mL/L — most effective; apply every 7 days | Mancozeb (Dithane M-45 75 WP) at 2.5 g/L — broad-spectrum contact protectant | Azoxystrobin (Amistar 25 SC) at 1 mL/L — systemic strobilurin, preventive + curative | Pyraclostrobin + Boscalid (Pristine WG) at 0.7 g/L — excellent Septoria efficacy | Difenoconazole (Score 250 EC) at 0.5 mL/L — systemic DMI for advanced infections | Copper oxychloride (50 WP) at 3 g/L — copper-based contact option | Trifloxystrobin (Flint 50 WG) at 0.1 g/L — translaminar strobilurin | Maintain strict 7-day spray interval during wet weather for full control

Treatment: Chlorothalonil (Bravo 720 SC) at 2 mL/L — most effective; apply every 7 days | Mancozeb (Dithane M-45 75 WP) at 2.5 g/L — broad-spectrum contact protectant | Azoxystrobin (Amistar 25 SC) at 1 mL/L — systemic strobilurin, preventive + curative | Pyraclostrobin + Boscalid (Pristine WG) at 0.7 g/L — excellent Septoria efficacy | Difenoconazole (Score 250 EC) at 0.5 mL/L — systemic DMI for advanced infections | Copper oxychloride (50 WP) at 3 g/L — copper-based contact option | Trifloxystrobin (Flint 50 WG) at 0.1 g/L — translaminar strobilurin | Maintain strict 7-day spray interval during wet weather for full control

Symptoms: Fine yellow stippling or speckling on upper leaf surface — bronze or silver sheen | Stippled areas coalesce causing large yellowish-bronze patches on leaves | Fine silken webbing on undersides of leaves and between leaves | Tiny moving reddish-brown or pale mites visible on leaf underside with hand lens | Leaves turn yellow to reddish-brown, dry up, and drop prematurely | Growing tips, flowers, and fruit surface may show webbing in heavy infestations | Defoliation of lower and middle canopy in severe outbreaks | Fruit may show light-colored russeting on surface in heavy infestations
Prevention: Scout plants twice weekly using a hand lens — detect colonies before they explode | Introduce or conserve natural predatory mites (Phytoseiulus persimilis, Neoseiulus californicus) | Avoid broad-spectrum insecticides that kill natural predator populations | Maintain adequate soil moisture — drought-stressed plants are the most susceptible | Reduce dusty conditions on field edges — dust suppresses natural predator mites | Remove and destroy heavily infested plant material before mites spread to adjacent plants | Use reflective silver mulches to deter mite movement into the crop | Manage weeds on field margins to eliminate alternate mite host plants
Treatment: Abamectin (Vertimec 1.8 EC) at 1 mL/L — highly effective; contact + translaminar activity | Spiromesifen (Oberon 240 SC) at 1 mL/L — acaricide effective on eggs and juveniles | Bifenazate (Floramite 240 SC) at 0.5 mL/L — fast knockdown, low resistance risk | Hexythiazox (Nissorun 10 WP) at 0.5 g/L — ovicide + larvicide, long residual | Etoxazole (Zeal 72 WSP) at 0.05 g/L — highly effective on eggs and larvae | Neem oil (Azadirachtin 0.03%) at 5 mL/L — organic repellent and growth disruptor | Fenbutatin oxide (Vendex 50 WP) at 1.5 g/L — organotin miticide | Rotate acaricide classes strictly with every application — mites develop resistance very rapidly

Symptoms: Circular to irregular brown lesions with concentric rings (target-board pattern) on leaves | Lesions up to 1 cm with yellow halos on older leaves | Distinct dark brown border with lighter tan-brown center | Small, dark, water-soaked spots on fruit that enlarge and become sunken | Fruit lesions develop concentric rings — tan-brown depressed spots | Stem lesions — dark brown elliptical cankers on petioles and stem | Premature defoliation of lower canopy | Dark sporulation visible in lesion centers under humid conditions (similar to early blight but lesions lack prominent yellow halo)
Prevention: Stake and prune plants to open the canopy and improve airflow | Avoid overhead irrigation — use drip systems | Maintain proper plant spacing — avoid overcrowding | Rotate crops with non-solanaceous plants between tomato seasons | Remove lower leaves showing first small brown lesions before they multiply | Scout fields regularly from transplanting through harvest | Remove and destroy all crop debris after harvest to eliminate inoculum source | Plant less-susceptible tomato varieties where available
Treatment: Chlorothalonil (Bravo 720 SC) at 2 mL/L — effective contact fungicide | Azoxystrobin (Amistar 25 SC) at 1 mL/L — strobilurin with systemic activity | Pyraclostrobin + Boscalid (Pristine WG) at 0.7 g/L — good curative efficacy | Tebuconazole (Folicur 250 EW) at 1 mL/L — systemic triazole | Fluxapyroxad + Pyraclostrobin (Merivon SC) at 0.8 mL/L — SDHI + strobilurin | Difenoconazole (Score 250 EC) at 0.5 mL/L — systemic DMI | Mancozeb (Dithane M-45 75 WP) at 2.5 g/L — broad-spectrum protectant | Maintain 10-day spray interval during humid conditions; rotate fungicide groups

Symptoms: Upward and inward curling of leaf margins — leaves form a cup or bowl shape | Leaflets reduced in size; leaves appear crinkled, thick, and leathery | Interveinal chlorosis — yellow discoloration between leaf veins | Stunting of entire plant — severely infected plants are markedly smaller | Flower drop and poor fruit set — infected plants are often unproductive | Fruit on infected plants may ripen unevenly or remain small | Purplish discoloration of leaf undersides in some strains | Young growing tips show most severe yellowing and distortion
Prevention: Plant TYLCV-resistant tomato varieties (Shanty, Forteza, Hazera hybrids) | Establish nurseries under 50-mesh insect-proof screens to block whitefly entry | Use yellow sticky traps to monitor whitefly (Bemisia tabaci) populations | Apply reflective silver mulch around plants to deter whitefly landing | Remove and destroy any plant showing curled yellowing leaves immediately | Maintain 50-metre buffer zones between tomato plantings and other host crops | Avoid planting near known infected or neglected solanaceous crops | Start whitefly control at nursery stage before transplanting
Treatment: Imidacloprid (Confidor 200 SL) at 0.5 mL/L — systemic neonicotinoid for whitefly control | Thiamethoxam (Actara 25 WG) at 0.3 g/L — systemic whitefly management | Spiromesifen (Oberon 240 SC) at 1 mL/L — controls whitefly nymphs on leaves | Pyriproxyfen (Admiral 100 EC) at 1 mL/L — insect growth regulator for juvenile whitefly | Buprofezin (Applaud 25 SC) at 2 mL/L — IGR disrupting whitefly nymph development | Neem oil (5 mL/L) — organic repellent and whitefly deterrent | Foliar micronutrient spray (zinc, iron) to support symptomatic plants | Note: Infected plants cannot be cured — remove and destroy to protect neighbouring plants

Symptoms: Mosaic pattern on leaves — irregular patches of light green, yellow, and dark green | Leaf blade distortion — leaves become narrow, twisted, fern-like (fernleaf symptom) | Mottled discoloration alternating between pale and dark green on same leaf | Stunted plant growth and reduced plant vigor | Blistering (raised and sunken areas) of leaf surface | Necrotic streaks on stems and petioles in severe ToMV infections | Internal fruit browning — brown vascular streaking inside fruit (brown wall symptom) | Uneven fruit ripening — blotchy ripening or yellow shoulders on fruit
Prevention: Plant ToMV-resistant tomato varieties carrying Tm-2 or Tm-2² resistance genes | Use certified virus-tested seed — ToMV is seed-transmitted | Treat seeds with 10% trisodium phosphate (TSP) solution for 15 minutes before sowing | Wash hands thoroughly with soap before handling any plants | Avoid smoking near tomato plants — tobacco carries closely related TMV | Disinfect all tools, stakes, and supports with 1% bleach or TSP solution | Remove and destroy any plant showing mosaic mottling or leaf distortion immediately | Do not use manure from animals fed on virus-infected plant material
Treatment: Imidacloprid (Confidor 200 SL) at 0.5 mL/L — systemic insecticide to control aphid and mechanical vectors | Mineral oil sprays (1–2%) — reduce aphid probing and virus transmission | Acibenzolar-S-methyl (Actigard 50 WG) at 0.05 g/L — SAR inducer to reduce symptom severity | Foliar NPK + micronutrient spray to maintain vigour of non-infected plants | Balanced irrigation and nutrition management to reduce overall plant stress | Remove infected plants promptly — reduces virus spread to healthy neighbours | Weed management to eliminate alternative host plants around the field | Note: No chemical cure exists for ToMV — management centres on containment and vector control

Symptoms: Mosaic pattern on leaves — irregular patches of light green, yellow, and dark green | Leaf blade distortion — leaves become narrow, twisted, fern-like (fernleaf symptom) | Mottled discoloration alternating between pale and dark green on same leaf | Stunted plant growth and reduced plant vigor | Blistering (raised and sunken areas) of leaf surface | Necrotic streaks on stems and petioles in severe ToMV infections | Internal fruit browning — brown vascular streaking inside fruit (brown wall symptom) | Uneven fruit ripening — blotchy ripening or yellow shoulders on fruit
